audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Exodus / Shemot presence-crisis text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for post-calf presence crisis, ornaments removed, tent outside camp, face-to-face speech, Moses' intercession for presence, glory request, and goodness/name proclamation preview.

verses.restored: YHWH spoke to Moses, Go, go up from here, you and the people whom you brought up from land of Egypt, to land that I swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, saying, To your seed I will give it.
I will send a messenger before you, and I will drive out Canaanite, Amorite, Hittite, Perizzite, Hivite, and Jebusite.
Go to land flowing with milk and honey; but I will not go up in your midst, for you are stiff-necked people, lest I consume you on way.
When the people heard this harmful word, they mourned, and no one put on ornaments.

YHWH said to Moses, Say to children of Israel, You are stiff-necked people; if one moment I go up in your midst, I would consume you. Now take down your ornaments from you, and I will know what I will do to you.
Children of Israel stripped themselves of ornaments from Mount Horeb onward.
Moses would take the tent and pitch it outside camp, far from camp, and called it tent of meeting; everyone seeking YHWH would go out to tent of meeting outside camp.
It happened when Moses went out to tent, all people rose and stood, each at opening of one's tent, and watched after Moses until he entered tent.
When Moses entered tent, pillar of cloud would descend and stand at opening of tent, and he would speak with Moses.
All the people saw pillar of cloud standing at opening of tent, and all the people rose and worshiped, each at opening of one's tent.
YHWH spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his companion. Then he returned to camp, but his attendant Joshua son of Nun, a young man, would not depart from midst of tent.

Moses said to YHWH, See, you say to me, Bring up this people, but you yourself have not made known to me whom you will send with me. Yet you said, I know you by name, and also you have found favor in my eyes.
Now please, if I have found favor in your eyes, make known to me your ways, so that I may know you, so that I may find favor in your eyes. And see that this nation is your people.
He said, My presence/face will go, and I will give you rest.
He said to him, If your presence/face is not going, do not bring us up from here.

For by what then shall it be known that I have found favor in your eyes, I and your people? Is it not in your going with us, so that we are distinguished, I and your people, from every people upon face of ground?
YHWH said to Moses, Even this matter that you spoke I will do, for you found favor in my eyes, and I know you by name.
He said, Please show me your glory.

He said, I will make all my goodness pass before your face, and I will call in name YHWH before you; I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and I will have compassion on whom I will have compassion.
He said, You are not able to see my face, for human shall not see me and live.
YHWH said, Look, a place is with me; you shall stand upon the rock.
It shall be, when my glory passes, I will put you in cleft of rock and cover my palm over you until I pass.
Then I will remove my palm, and you shall see my back/afterward, but my face shall not be seen.

translation_notes: Presence/face (panim) is central: Moses refuses a promised land without YHWH's presence.
Tent outside camp marks presence after rupture before tabernacle completion; seeking YHWH requires going out.
Face-to-face in v.11 and not seeing face in v.20 are held in narrative tension; preserve both rather than harmonizing too quickly.
Moses asks to know YHWH's ways and see glory; YHWH answers with goodness, name, grace, and compassion.
Land promise language remains source-preserved with ethical audit flags from prior chapters.
safety_note: Do not use presence language to erase accountability after idolatry. The chapter holds rupture, mourning, intercession, and mercy together; Moses seeks presence for the whole people, not private spiritual status.
